coredata.dc:title: A high-sensitivity electrochemical aptasensor of carcinoembryonic antigen based on graphene quantum dots-ionic liquid-nafion nanomatrix and DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jing-Yi
Zhao, Lang
Lei, Wan
Wen, Wei
Wang, Yi-Jia
Bao, Ting
Xiong, Hua-Yu
Zhang, Xiu-Hua
Wang, Sheng-Fu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have developed an electrochemical aptasensor for high-sensitivity determination of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) based on lead ion (Pb2+)-dependent DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification and graphene quantum dot-ionic liquid-nafion (GQDs-IL-NF) composite film. We designed hairpin DNA containing CEA-specific aptamers and DNAzyme chains. In the presence of CEA, hairpin DNA recognized the target and performed a DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification reaction to yield a large number of single-stranded DNA. The GQDs-IL-NF composite film was immobilized on the glassy carbon electrode for the interaction with single-stranded DNA through noncovalent π-π stacking interaction. Therefore, the methylene blue-labeled substrate DNA (MB-substrate) was fixed on the electrode and exhibited an initial electrochemical signal. Under optimal conditions, the response current change was proportional to the concentration of CEA, demonstrating a wide linear range from 0.5fgmL−1 to 0.5ngmL−1, with a low detection limit of 0.34fgmL−1. Furthermore, the proposed aptasensor was successfully applied in determining CEA in serum samples, showing its superior prospects in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
DNAzyme
Ionic liquid
Electrochemical aptasensor
Carcinoembryonic antigen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction and design of the electrochemical aptasensor
Characterization of the GQDs-Il-Nafion composite film
Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the aptamer sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the GQDs-IL-NF nanomatrix and DNAzyme-assisted recycling of target-aptamer complex for sensitive electrochemical detection of CEA.
(A) CV of (a) GCE/NF, (b) GCE/IL-NF and (c) GCE/GQDs-IL-NF in PBS containing 0.5mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4-. (B) TEM images of GQDs. Inset: size distributions of GQDs.
EIS (A) and CV (B) of (a) bare GCE, (b) GCE/GQDs-IL-NF, (c) GCE/GQDs-IL-NF/MB-substrate and (d) GCE/GQDs-IL-NF/MB-substrate/auxiliary DNA in PBS containing 0.5mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4-.

Effects of (A) the concentration of Pb2+, (B) the concentration of substrate chain, (C) recycling time of aptamer and (D) capture time of auxiliary DNA. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

(A) Current response of MB-substrate/GQDs-IL-NF/GCE after incubation with different concentration of CEA, CEA concentration (a~g): 0.5ngmL−1, 50pgmL−1, 5pgmL−1, 0.5pgmL−1, 50fgmL−1, 5fgmL−1, 0.5fgmL−1 (B) Variation of the peak current value versus a concentration of CEA. Inset: the linear relationship of ΔI versus the logarithm of CEA concentration. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

Selectivity of the proposed electrochemical biosensor to blank sample, 100pgmL−1 of CEA, 10ngmL−1 of PSA, 10ngmL−1 of BSA, 10ngmL−1 of MUC1 and mixture solution (containing 100pgmL−1 CEA, 10ngmL−1 PSA, 10ngmL−1 BSA, and 10ngmL−1 MUC1), respectively. Error bars are obtained based on three independent measurements.

head.ce:title: A high-sensitivity electrochemical aptasensor of carcinoembryonic antigen based on graphene quantum dots-ionic liquid-nafion nanomatrix and DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification strategy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry of Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules&College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have developed an electrochemical aptasensor for high-sensitivity determination of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) based on lead ion (Pb2+)-dependent DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification and graphene quantum dot-ionic liquid-nafion (GQDs-IL-NF) composite film. We designed hairpin DNA containing CEA-specific aptamers and DNAzyme chains. In the presence of CEA, hairpin DNA recognized the target and performed a DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification reaction to yield a large number of single-stranded DNA. The GQDs-IL-NF composite film was immobilized on the glassy carbon electrode for the interaction with single-stranded DNA through noncovalent π-π stacking interaction. Therefore, the methylene blue-labeled substrate DNA (MB-substrate) was fixed on the electrode and exhibited an initial electrochemical signal. Under optimal conditions, the response current change was proportional to the concentration of CEA, demonstrating a wide linear range from 0.5fgmL−1 to 0.5ngmL−1, with a low detection limit of 0.34fgmL−1. Furthermore, the proposed aptasensor was successfully applied in determining CEA in serum samples, showing its superior prospects in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of modern society's greatest challenges in terms of deadly diseases. As such, there is extensive research into this class of diseases. Among the many areas of study, the detection of protein biomarkers has very important significance in exploring early diagnosis, elaborating the rule of pathological changes, and so on. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a glycoprotein formed by many malignant tissues that can reflect the existence of a wide variety of tumors (Wen et al., 2016). CEA is also one of the most promising tumor markers for diagnosing cancer, monitoring and prognosis estimation, and can indicate the curative effect of various cancers (e.g. colorectal cancer, breast cancer and lung cancer) (Miao et al., 2016). Therefore, developing a convenient, rapid and sensitive analytical method for CEA detection in serum is of great importance. The level of CEA is often found in abnormally high concentrations in the serum of patients with malignancies, particularly epithelial tumors. A CEA level of 5.0ngmL−1 in human serum is commonly used cutoff point for distinguishing abnormal from normal level. Serial measurement of CEA levels in patients with colorectal cancer can monitor different stages of disease, and detect recurrences early after surgery (Fletcher, 1986). However, the concentration of CEA in human serum is very low, especially in the initial stage of cancer progression. Therefore, it is necessary to establish a highly sensitive method for protein detection (Chang et al., 2010). In particular, enzyme-based signal amplification strategy has attracted a lot of interest in biosensing due to its high specificity, sensitivity and effective catalytic activity (Bao et al., 2015; Ge et al., 2016). Although promising, the use of protein enzymatic signal amplification is limited due to the high cost and poor enzyme stability.

In recent years, DNAzyme has received great attention as a new type of signal amplification recognition tool in the development of protein detection (Liu et al., 2016; Xia et al., 2015). It is a single-stranded oligodeoxynucleotide capable of catalyzing a specific chemical reaction (Shen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). Compared to protein enzyme, it benefits from lower cost, better thermal stability and ease of synthesis (Wang et al., 2016, 2015). Since the DNAzyme can be complementary to a specific substrate sequence, the substrate chain is cleaved into two fragments when metal ions are present as cofactors (Cui et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2007). In addition, the metal ion-dependent DNAzyme can undergo multiple conversions without loss of binding capacity and activity. Thus it can be subjected to a target cycling signal amplification reaction (Yang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2015). DNAzyme signal amplification strategy provides a good method for high-sensitivity determination of proteins through the target recirculation process.

Carbon materials modified electrodes have been widely used as electrochemical sensors. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as the most dazzling member of the family of carbon materials, have ignited great attention in the field of modified electrodes (Hu et al., 2016; J. Li et al., 2016; L. Wang et al., 2013; X. Wang et al., 2013). GQDs, as a type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial, are widely used in electrochemical detection, because of their high thermal conductivity, large surface area and high mobility of charge carriers (Wang et al., 2014, 2017; Zheng et al., 2015). In addition, GQDs also have some features of graphene, which can combine with single-stranded DNA through the π-π stacking. But they are unable to combine with rigid double-stranded DNA (Chau et al., 2016; He et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2013). Nevertheless, GQDs have great solubility in aqueous solution, so they are not easily fixed on the surface of electrode. Nafion (NF), as a sulfonated ion exchange polymer, is a good choice for an excellent electrode matrix material due to its distinct electrochemical properties such as chemical inertness, outstanding antifouling capacity, great permeability to cations and good biocompatibility (Chaiyo et al., 2016; Gong et al., 2017;). In addition, NF can fix the carbon nanomaterial on the surface of electrode because of its hydrophobicity. It can also prevent the aggregation between the carbon nanomaterials (Xie et al., 2017; Zarei and Helli, 2015). However, pure NF film suffers from low conductivity which hinders electron transport (Y. Li et al., 2016; X. Zhang et al., 2016). A conductive material is often used in combination with NF to overcome this shortcoming. As an electrochemical material with excellent ionic conductivity and wide electrochemical window, ionic liquid (IL) is widely used as an electrode modifier in the manufacture of sensors (Chen et al., 2013; L. Wang et al., 2013; X. Wang et al., 2013; H. Zhang et al., 2016). The integration of GQDs, IL and NF could elicit synergistic effects in the electrochemical applications.

In this paper, we developed a novel electrochemical aptasensor based on Pb2+-dependent DNAzyme assisted signal amplification and GQDs-IL-NF composite film for detection of CEA. With good conductivity, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, GQDs-IL-NF composite films were employed as the carrier for DNA immobilization through non-covalent π-π stacking interaction. Due to Pb2+-dependent DNAzyme assisted target recycling signal amplification, it provided high electrochemical response signals. The designed aptamer sensor for CEA detection showed high selectivity, good reproducibility and acceptable stability. Herein, we proposed a promising method for the determination of CEA with intriguing prospects for clinical applications.

Citric acid monohydrate, Nafion (5%, v/v) and ionic liquid 1-butyl-2,3-dimethylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Saturated solutions of sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), and Lead nitrate were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent (China). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), mucin 1 protein (MUC1) and prostate specific antigen (PSA) were purchased from Linc-Bio Science Co., Ltd (China). 10mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was obtained using Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 and used as supporting electrolyte. 20mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) containing 5mM MgCl2, 140mM NaCl was used to dissolve DNA. All buffer solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water (18.25MΩcm) from an Aquapro water purification system (China).

Electrochemical experiments were implemented using a CHI660C electrochemical system (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China). In this experiment, three-electrode electrochemical system was used with the platinum as the auxiliary electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and GQDs-IL-NF composite film modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as working electrode. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) was performed on a FEI Tecnai G20 (USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment was recorded by Malvern ZS90 (UK).

The preparation procedure of aptamer signal probes is shown in Fig. 1. First, the hairpin DNA was heated to 95°C for 5min in a water bath, and lentamente cooled to room temperature to form a stem-loop DNA structure and stored at 4°C. To detect CEA, the hairpin DNA (10μL, 1μM) was pretreated at 37°C in a water bath for 1h and then 10μL of different concentrations of CEA were added. The mixture was incubated in a 37°C for 1h. Cleavage reaction was performed by adding of the MB-substrate (5μL, 3μM) and pb2+ (15μL, 10μM) to the above solution (50μL) and incubated at 37°C for 1h.

The GQDs were synthesized from pyrolyzing citric acid following the previously reported procedure (Dong et al., 2012). The obtained solutions were stored at 4°C. The manufacturing procedure for the GQDs-IL-NF modified GCE is briefly described as follow. First, a bare GCE was polished by 0.05µm alumina slurry. And the GCE was sequentially sonicated with ultra-pure ethanol and water for 5min to remove the remaining alumina powder and then the cleaned GCE was allowed to dry at 25°C. 2mL of 0.2wt% Nafion, 100μL of 1% ionic liquid, and 1mL 1mg/mL GQDs solution were mixed homogenously and sonicated for 30min. Finally, 5μL of the GQDs-IL-NF composite was drop-coated onto the surface of the cleaned GCE and dried at room temperature.

The GQDs-IL-NF nanocomposite film modified GCE was immersed in aptamer solution and incubated at 25°C for 12h. It was washed in buffer solution to remove the physically adsorbed aptamers. Finally, the electrodes were incubated in 2μM of auxiliary DNA for 80min and washed with PBS solution after fabrication. Electrochemical behaviors of the each step modification of the aptamer sensor were studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammograms (CV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and design of the electrochemical aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: This electrochemical aptasensor for CEA detection involves Pb2+-dependent DNAzyme assisted target recycling signal amplification and GQDs-IL-NF composite film as a substrate for DNA immobilization. As shown in Fig. 1, we designed a hairpin DNA containing the CEA aptamer sequence and DNAzyme chain, respectively. In the presence of CEA, the hairpin DNA recognized the target and formed a CEA-aptamer complex. The exposure DNAzyme sequence hybridized with methylene blue (MB, signal molecule) labeled substrate chain introduced a specific nicking site for DNAzyme. In the presence of Pb2+, the complementary pair of double-stranded DNA was cleaved and the substrate chain was cleaved into two fragments. The CEA-aptamer complex was released and a DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification reaction was performed, yielding a large number of MB-substrates. Then, the GQDs-IL-NF composite film was dispersed on the surface of GCE for the sake of interacting with MB-substrates through noncovalent π-π stacking interaction, producing a large initial electrochemical signal. In order to avoid the substrate chain adsorbing on the electrode surface and causing false positive results, we designed the auxiliary DNA, which can be completely matched to the MB-substrate. The presence of auxiliary DNA triggers the hybridization reaction to form double-stranded DNA, and thus the MB-substrate released from the surface of electrode, resulting in a low electrochemical signal. The magnitude of the signal degradation is related to the concentration of CEA in the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the GQDs-Il-Nafion composite film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A showed CVs of carbon electrode modified with NF (curve a), IL-NF (curve b) and GQDs-IL-NF (curve c) in 10mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in the potential range between −0.2 ~ 0.6V with a scanning rate of 0.1Vs−1. When the bare electrode was fixed with NF film, the redox peaks were not obtained. This phenomenon indicated that NF was greatly hindered electron transfer and was almost insulated (X. Zhang et al., 2016) (curve a). After the electrode was fixed with IL-NF composite film (curve b), the redox current increased significantly. The peaks can be attributed to the electrical conductivity of ionic liquid. A couple of redox peaks of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (curve c) were observed when GQDs-IL-NF composite film was fabricated on the bare electrode. It showed that GQDs can further increase the electrical conductivity of the sensing interface. The TEM graph of GQDs was shown in Fig. 2B. The diameters of the GQDs were distributed mainly over the range of 1.5–5nm with an average diameter of 3.3nm.

CV and EIS effectively investigated the stepwise assembly process of electrode measurements. EIS curves were shown in Fig. 3A. The semicircle diameter at Nyquist plots of the EIS is equal to the value of electron-transfer resistance, Ret. The bare GCE exhibited a straight line (curve a), which indicated a fast electron-transfer on the bare GCE surface. After the GQDs-IL-NF composite film was fabricated onto the surface of GCE, the Ret was slightly increased due to the introduction of insulated NF film (curve b). When MB-substrate self-assembled on the GQDs-IL-NF composite film modified GCE, the Ret increased significantly (curve c), because the oligonucleotides were non-conductive. Finally, when the electrode was treated with auxiliary DNA, the auxiliary DNA could be completely matched to MB-substrate. The formed double-stranded DNA detached from electrode surface, and the Ret was certainly decreased due to complete complementation of the paired duplexes off the electrode after DNA hybridization (curve d). These results can also be verified by those obtained from CV measurements (Fig. 3B). As is shown in curve a, a pair of well-defined redox peaks of the redox couple of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− was observed at bare GCE. When GQDs-IL-NF composite film was immobilized on the GCE, the decrease of CV peak current was observed due to the electron-transfer resistance increase (curve b). After introduced MB-substrate to the GQDs-IL-NF composite film modified GCE, the decrease of CV peak current was obtained (curve c). Additionally, the CV peak current increased after auxiliary DNA hybridized with the MB-substrate (curve d).

To obtain high sensitivity for CEA detection, the experimental conditions were optimized in terms of lead ion concentration, substrate chain concentration, recycle time of aptamer and capture time of auxiliary DNA on the electrode. With the increase of the concentration of Pb2+, the cleavage rate of the substrate chain increased. As shown in Fig. 4A, the signal intensity increased with the increasing reaction concentration of Pb2+ and tended to a steady value at 10μM, indicating that the DNA cleavage activity of DNAzyme was induced by Pb2+. Thus, 10μM Pb2+ was selected for the following studies. The concentration of the substrate chain influenced the degree of the signal amplification at recycle process. Low concentrations of the substrate chain probe may result in insufficient cycling, resulting low sensitivity of the biosensor. Excessive amounts of the substrate chain probe may lead to a high background signal, resulting in limited sensing performance. As shown in Fig. 4B, different concentrations of substrate chain from 1 to 5μM were investigated, and 3μM was selected as the optimal concentration.

The recycling time of aptamer affected the analytical performance of biosensor. As presented in Fig. 4C, the electrochemical signal increased with the extension of recycle time of aptamer and reached a plateau after 60min thus 60min was selected as the optimal digestion time. Besides, as shown in Fig. 4D, the capture time of auxiliary DNA on the electrode caused great effect to the performance of the proposed biosensor. The electrochemical signal intensity reached its maximum value with increasing capture time up to 80min before leveling off, indicating the saturation of hybridization between the MB-substrate and auxiliary DNA. Therefore, a capture time of auxiliary DNA of 80min was selected in the experiments.

In order to study the proposed platform, the prepared electrochemical biosensor was used to detect different concentrations of CEA in 0.1M PBS buffer under optimal sensing conditions. Upon increasing concentration of CEA, we observed a large increase current response of MB-substrate/GQDs-IL-NF/GCE, because the increasing numbers of MB-substrate were yielded through a target-induced DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification process, and then more MB-substrates were captured on the surface of GQDs-IL-NF composite film. Fig. 5A displayed the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) currents of different concentrations of CEA. Fig. 5B plotted the variation of current intensity (ΔI, the current variation with and without auxiliary DNA) and concentration of CEA. ΔI exhibited excellent linear relation with the logarithm of CEA concentration ranging from 0.5fgmL−1 to 0.5ngmL−1. The regression equation was ΔI = 8.916*10−7 + 1.158*10−7 lgc (correlation coefficient was 0.9981). A detection limit for CEA was obtained to be 0.34fgmL−1 (signal-to-noise of 3). Compared with those previously reported biosensors of CEA (Table S1 in the Supporting information), the proposed electrochemical biosensor has a desirable linear range and high sensitivity because of the DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification strategy and excellent properties of GQDs-IL-NF composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the aptamer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of CEA may be interfered by a variety of protein analogues. To evaluate the selectivity, the biosensor was tested with other common protein interferences (such as BSA, PSA, MUC1) in the same conditions. Fig. 6 showed the different DPV current intensity responses of the proposed sensing system after the addition of a blank sample, 100pgmL−1 of CEA, 10ngmL−1 of PSA, 10ngmL−1 of BSA, 10ngmL−1 of MUC1 and mixture solution (containing 100pgmL−1 CEA, 10ngmL−1 PSA, 10ngmL−1 BSA, and 10ngmL−1 MUC1). A negligible response was observed when the biosensor was challenged with above possible interference protein even at a 100-fold higher concentration than CEA. When the biosensor was incubated with 100pgmL−1 of CEA, a high DPV response was achieved. This could be attributed to the specificity of the CEA aptamer. All these results indicated that the proposed biosensor had a great specificity for CEA.

The reproducibility of the biosensor was tested using four electrodes to detect the same concentration of CEA (100pgmL−1) under identical conditions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 4.4%. When the same electrode was used to test reproducibility for four times at a certain concentration of CEA (100pgmL−1), the RSD was 5.2%. The stability of the biosensor was examined by monitoring the response of CEA (100pgmL−1) after an interval of one week at 4°C. Only about 6.3% degeneration in the current response was observed if the same electrode was used beyond a week. These results demonstrated that the stability and reproducibility of the biosensor were acceptable.

To investigate the feasibility of the developed electrochemical biosensor, recovery experiments were carried out by adding samples of healthy human serum that were diluted 10-fold to various concentrations of CEA. As anticipated from Table S2 in the supporting information, the recoveries were ranging from 96.8% to 104.3% and the RSD varied from 1.9% to 2.7%, respectively. This result demonstrated that the aptasensor can be used to determine CEA in real biological samples.

In summary, a novel electrochemical biosensor based on GQDs-IL-NF composite film and Pb2+-dependent DNAzyme-assisted recycling for the determination of CEA was reported. The greatly enhanced sensitivity of the aptasensor was relied on the signal amplification strategy. First, Pb2+-dependent DNAzyme led to the recycling of target-aptamer complex causing the continuous cleavage of the substrate chain and amplified the electrochemical signal. Besides, the GQDs-IL-NF composite film was fabricated on electrode for the first time, which led to the ssDNA adsorbed on GQDs-IL-NF modified GCE via the π–π stacking interactions. Under the optimum conditions, the electrochemical biosensor detected CEA within the concentration ranges from 0.5fgmL−1 to 0.5ngmL−1, with a detection limit of 0.34fgmL−1. The aptasensor for CEA detection showed high selectivity, good reproducibility and acceptable stability. Therefore, we propose a promising method for the determination of CEA with intriguing prospects for clinical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405035, 21475032), the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (No. SKLACLS1501), and the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (No. Z2015021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.07.036.
